title: Why More Context Makes Your Agent Dumber and What to Do About It — Nupur Sharma, Qodo
summary: Long context windows do not fix agent degradation in the middle of documents, a phenomenon called the U curve. Nupur Sharma presents iterative retrieval, hierarchical summarization, and self correction as solutions, along with a hybrid model approach where high-reasoning models handle discovery and lighter models perform validation.
writeup: A practitioner from Kodo (an agentic code-review company) presents hard-won failure modes observed while building production multi-agent systems, grounding each in concrete benchmarking work. The talk moves through three structural problems: context loss following a U-curve where middle-of-window information is silently discarded, an orchestration paradox where capable models enter infinite method-selection loops instead of executing, and task-overwhelm in monolithic agents that silently drop goals as context grows. For each failure, a practical mitigation is named and assessed, culminating in the 80/20 hybrid model-tier strategy and mixture-of-agents decomposition.
key_points: LLMs attending to context follow a U-curve: inputs at the start and end of the context window are retained, but middle-context content (Jira tickets, MCP references, intermediate retrieval results) is silently purged, so dumping a full codebase or tool list into one context window does not guarantee that content influences the output.

Four context-optimization strategies are assessed on input cost versus scale: context engines (good ranking logic but indexing breaks down past 600-700 repositories), hierarchical summarization (file-level summaries reduce search scope but require LLM processing on every file change), knowledge graphs (handles complex cross-repo dependencies well but demands high upfront developer investment), and iterative retrieval (low developer input, library-card-style indexing, works well for most non-product-company agent builds).

The orchestration paradox describes a specific failure mode where high-capability models such as Opus enter a self-challenging research loop, spending the majority of API tokens evaluating which method to use rather than executing, often without ever producing a result.

The 80/20 hybrid approach addresses the orchestration paradox by reserving the latest reasoning models for the open-ended 80% of a task (planning, discovery, tool selection) while routing the deterministic 20% (critic nodes, summarization, final validation) to smaller, cheaper models with hard gates; counter limits (4-5 iterations) and timeout cutoffs (5 minutes) bound the research phase.

Assigning four or more tasks to a single large-context agent causes silent goal-dropping: the agent produces strong results for two tasks while the remaining two are lost in the middle context, making mixture-of-agents (small specialist agents per task, outputs merged by an orchestrator) the structural remedy.
